critic_raw:
This design trains a Double DQN agent for adaptive traffic signal control and evaluates it against fixed-time and adaptive baselines on real-world traffic data.

The most critical methodological problem is the simulation-to-reality gap. The DQN agent is trained 'in a deterministic traffic simulator initialized with statistical properties from the training data.' The evaluation then claims to test on real-world traffic data from months 16–18. The agent is trained in a simulator, but evaluated in reality — yet the design describes the evaluation as a standard offline temporal hold-out. The agent is not actually deployed and controlling real signals during months 16–18; instead, it appears to be applying its learned policy to the historical traffic data. This means the RL reward signal (average wait time) must be computed from historical loop detector data, not from the agent's actual real-time control. Historical data reflects decisions made by the existing fixed-time or adaptive controller, not the DQN agent's decisions. Applying a DQN policy to historical detector readings without simulating the counterfactual traffic flows that would have resulted from different signal choices is a fundamental methodological error — it evaluates the policy as if traffic behavior is independent of the signal timing decisions made.

The reward function is 'negative average wait time in the cycle plus a small penalty for phase transitions.' Wait times during training are computed from the simulator. Wait times during evaluation are computed from 'detector occupancy data using standard queueing approximations.' If the queueing approximation used at evaluation time differs systematically from the simulator's wait time model, the agent's reward signal during training is not aligned with the evaluation metric. The agent optimized for simulator-derived wait times may not optimize queue-approximation-derived wait times, and any improvement claimed could be a measurement inconsistency artifact.

The design uses only 3–5 intersections. Traffic control is a network problem: optimizing signal timing at one intersection affects vehicle arrivals and queuing at adjacent intersections. Evaluating a single-intersection DQN agent on 3–5 isolated intersections ignores network-level effects. More critically, if the test intersections are adjacent to each other, the DQN agents at different intersections are interacting through traffic flows that are not modeled in the single-intersection RL formulation. The claimed wait-time reduction may be achieved by pushing queues to adjacent intersections rather than genuinely reducing system-level delay.
all_issues_raised: The DQN agent is trained in a simulator but evaluated by applying its policy to historical traffic data; historical data reflects the existing controller's decisions, not the DQN's counterfactual control actions — this makes the evaluation a policy simulation on historical inputs, not a genuine counterfactual traffic outcome comparison.

Simulator-derived wait times (the training reward) and queueing-approximation-derived wait times (the evaluation metric) are computed by different methods; systematic differences between these models mean the agent optimized for one metric is not guaranteed to score well on the other.

Single-intersection RL optimization evaluated at 3-5 intersections ignores network effects: improving wait time at one intersection may displace queues to adjacent intersections, and if tested intersections are geographically proximate, this displacement masquerades as system-level improvement.
all_issues_adjudicated: The DQN agent is trained in a simulator but evaluated by applying its policy to historical traffic data; historical data reflects the existing controller's decisions, not the DQN's counterfactual control actions — this makes the evaluation a policy simulation on historical inputs, not a genuine counterfactual traffic outcome comparison.

Simulator-derived wait times (the training reward) and queueing-approximation-derived wait times (the evaluation metric) are computed by different methods; systematic differences between these models mean the agent optimized for one metric is not guaranteed to score well on the other.

Single-intersection RL optimization evaluated at 3-5 intersections ignores network effects: improving wait time at one intersection may displace queues to adjacent intersections, and if tested intersections are geographically proximate, this displacement masquerades as system-level improvement.
